---
title: "I — Electrical foundations"
description: "Safe observation, circuit models, measurement, and dynamic behavior."
page-role: part
part-id: foundations
---

The first part establishes the habits on which every later result depends:
control the hazard, define what is being measured, choose reference directions,
state the model, and compare its prediction with evidence.

## Part question

How can a physical electrical system, a circuit model, and a set of measurements
be made to tell the same defensible story?

## Entry knowledge

No prior circuit theory is assumed. Basic algebra and scientific notation are
helpful; use [M01](../appendices/m01-algebra-units-complex.qmd) when units,
graphs, or equation manipulation need review. Practical work begins only with
current-limited extra-low-voltage systems and locally approved procedures.

## Learning arc

| Chapters | Development |
|---|---|
| F01–F02 | establish safe observation and define charge, voltage, and current |
| F03–F05 | model sources, loads, networks, energy, power, and heat |
| F06 | decide whether a measurement or debugging claim is trustworthy |
| F07–F08 | extend static models to stored energy, transients, sinusoids, and frequency response |

## Exit capability

You should be able to inspect and energize an approved low-energy circuit in
stages, predict its order of magnitude, measure without silently changing the
quantity of interest, reconcile a model with uncertainty, and stop when the
evidence or safety boundary is inadequate. Those capabilities feed every later
part; they are not preliminaries to discard.

The aligned practical sequence is L01–L05. Project M1 integrates the part into
a qualified energy-and-light measurement claim; see the
[practice and project spine](../roadmap.qmd#practice-and-project-spine).
